title: React Lead Engineer Expert Assessment
description: High-level evaluation for Lead/Staff roles. Focuses on system architecture, library design, reconciliation internals, and organizational performance strategies.

questions.text: In React 18, how does the 'Lanes' model improve upon the previous expiration-based priority system?
options: It uses a bitmask to allow multiple concurrent categories of work to be tracked and updated independently.
It forces all updates into a single linear queue to prevent race conditions.
It removes the concept of priority entirely in favor of first-in-first-out.
It only allows one lane per component subtree.
questions.explanation: Lanes allow React to be more granular, enabling 'entanglements' and the ability to skip certain types of work while processing others without losing track of their priority.

questions.text: What is the primary technical trade-off when choosing between 'Runtime CSS-in-JS' (e.g., Styled Components) and 'Zero-Runtime CSS-in-JS' (e.g., Panda CSS, Vanilla Extract) for a Lead?
options: Zero-runtime libraries have better dynamic theme support during runtime.
Runtime libraries simplify the build pipeline but add overhead to the 'main thread' during reconciliation.
Runtime libraries are faster for initial page load.
Zero-runtime libraries cannot use media queries.
questions.explanation: Runtime libraries must parse and inject styles during execution, which can block the main thread during heavy re-renders, unlike zero-runtime which move that work to the build step.

questions.text: How does 'Selective Hydration' in React 18 improve Time to Interactive (TTI)?
options: It hydrates the entire page at once but only after all images load.
It allows React to start hydrating parts of the page that the user interacts with first, even if other parts are still loading.
It disables hydration for mobile devices to save battery.
It converts client components to server components automatically.
questions.explanation: By wrapping components in Suspense, React can prioritize hydrating the specific chunk the user clicks on, rather than waiting for the full tree.

questions.text: When designing a large-scale Design System, what is the 'Compound Component' pattern's primary architectural advantage?
options: It reduces the total number of components in the library.
It provides a declarative API that manages internal state implicitly while allowing consumer flexibility in layout.
It makes the components render faster than standard props.
It prevents the use of the Context API.
questions.explanation: Compound components (e.g., Select/Select.Option) share state via Context, allowing developers to reorder or wrap sub-elements without 'prop drilling' every configuration.

questions.text: What is the consequence of 'Tearing' in a React application?
options: The CSS layout breaks and elements overlap.
Different components show different values for the same external state during a single render pass.
The application crashes with a 'Memory Leak' error.
The Virtual DOM fails to update the real DOM.
questions.explanation: Tearing occurs in concurrent rendering when an external store is mutated mid-render, leading to visual inconsistency across the UI.

questions.text: Which 'Render Phase' Hook is specifically designed for library authors to read layout before any other effects run?
options: useEffect
useLayoutEffect
useInsertionEffect
useMemo
questions.explanation: useInsertionEffect is the only hook that fires before useLayoutEffect, intended specifically for injecting dynamic styles.

questions.text: As a Lead, when would you recommend 'Micro-Frontends' over a Monolith React SPA?
options: When you want the fastest possible performance for the end user.
When multiple autonomous teams need to deploy independent features without coordinating release cycles.
When the application has fewer than 5 routes.
When you want to share state between all components using a single Redux store.
questions.explanation: Micro-frontends are an organizational solution for scaling teams, often at the cost of some performance and complexity.

questions.text: What is 'Partial Hydration' (Islands Architecture) as popularized by frameworks like Astro?
options: Only hydrating the top 50% of the viewport.
Sending zero JavaScript to the client by default and only hydrating specific interactive components.
Using only half of the available CPU cores for rendering.
Hydrating the app only when the user is logged in.
questions.explanation: Islands architecture minimizes JS by treating the page as static HTML with small 'islands' of interactivity.

questions.text: How does `useSyncExternalStore` solve the problem of tearing?
options: By making all external stores asynchronous.
By forcing a synchronous re-render whenever the external store changes, bypassing concurrent features for that update.
By creating a deep clone of the store on every render.
By moving the store into the React state.
questions.explanation: It provides a bridge that allows React to treat external data as a consistent source of truth, effectively opting out of time-slicing for that specific data.

questions.text: In a Staff/Lead role, what is the primary metric used to justify a large-scale React version upgrade to stakeholders?
options: Developer Satisfaction scores.
Improvement in Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP) and maintenance cost reduction.
Number of lines of code deleted.
The release of new decorative JSX features.
questions.explanation: Leads must tie technical debt and upgrades to business outcomes like user experience (Web Vitals) and velocity (maintenance).

questions.text: What is the 'Offscreen' (now `Activity`) API intended for?
options: Running code in a Web Worker.
Preserving component state and DOM while hiding the UI, similar to tab switching in a browser.
Tracking if a user has moved to a different tab.
Rendering components only when they are in the viewport.
questions.explanation: It allows React to deprioritize rendering for hidden components while maintaining their state, enabling instant switching.

questions.text: Which design pattern is best for ensuring a heavy data-fetching component doesn't block the UI during initial mount?
options: Fetch-on-render (useEffect).
Fetch-then-render (Promise.all).
Render-as-you-fetch (Suspense).
Client-side redirects.
questions.explanation: Render-as-you-fetch allows React to start rendering the skeleton/fallback immediately while the data request is in flight.

questions.text: What is the risk of using 'Index' as a key in a list that can be filtered or sorted?
options: React will throw a runtime error.
It can cause incorrect component state to be mapped to the wrong UI elements after a reorder.
It makes the list immutable.
It increases the bundle size.
questions.explanation: Since React uses keys to track identities, reusing the same index for different data items leads to 'ghost' state from the previous item.

questions.text: How do 'React Server Components' handle interactivity?
options: They use the `onClick` prop directly on the server.
They don't; they must import and render 'Client Components' for any interactive parts.
They use a specialized 'server-click' attribute.
By automatically converting to client components when clicked.
questions.explanation: RSCs are zero-bundle-size and non-interactive. They act as the data-fetching layer that passes props to Client Components.

questions.text: What is the primary role of a 'Bailout' in the React render process?
options: Canceling a render when an error occurs.
Skipping the rendering of a component and its subtree when its inputs (props/state) are identical.
Exiting the application when memory is low.
Reverting to the previous version of the app.
questions.explanation: Bailouts are critical for performance, ensuring React doesn't waste CPU cycles on unchanged parts of the tree.

questions.text: Which standard is used by React Server Components to stream data to the client?
options: WebSockets
HTTP/2 Server Push
ReadableStreams (sending chunks of a JSON-like format)
FTP
questions.explanation: RSCs use a specialized stream format that allows the client to begin rendering parts of the UI before the full response is received.

questions.text: In a large React project, why would a Lead implement a 'Custom Lint Rule' for the dependency array of useEffect?
options: To enforce alphabetical order.
To prevent developers from omitting reactive values that cause stale closures.
To limit the number of dependencies to 3.
To force the use of refs only.
questions.explanation: Stale closures are one of the most common and difficult-to-debug issues in React; automated linting is the best defense.

questions.text: What is the 'Prop-in-State' anti-pattern?
options: Passing a state setter as a prop.
Mirroring a prop in local state, which leads to the state becoming out of sync when the prop changes.
Using state to generate a prop.
Passing a prop to a child component.
questions.explanation: Leads usually advocate for 'Derived State' instead of copying props into state to maintain a single source of truth.

questions.text: What does 'Yielding' mean in the context of the Fiber scheduler?
options: The component returns a value.
React pauses its work to allow the browser to handle high-priority tasks like user input or animation.
The application gives up and shows an error.
Allowing a child component to update before its parent.
questions.explanation: Time-slicing (yielding) is what makes the UI feel smooth even when React is performing heavy calculations.

questions.text: Which optimization technique involves rendering a component only when it enters the browser viewport?
options: Memoization
Virtualization (Windowing)
Code Splitting
Hydration
questions.explanation: Virtualization keeps the DOM small by recycling nodes or only mounting what is visible.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of `useId` in a micro-frontend environment?
options: It creates a globally unique ID across different applications on the same page.
It generates stable IDs that prevent collisions between different MFE instances during hydration.
It identifies which MFE is currently active.
It measures the performance of an MFE.
questions.explanation: useId is stable across SSR/CSR and prevents different MFEs from generating clashing IDs for forms/accessibility.

questions.text: In React 18, what is the default behavior of `Error Boundaries` regarding concurrent rendering?
options: They stop the whole app immediately.
They can catch errors in the render phase, and React will attempt to 'recover' by rendering the fallback for that boundary.
They only work for class components.
They catch network errors automatically.
questions.explanation: Error boundaries are integrated into the fiber loop, allowing React to discard 'bad' work and show an alternative UI gracefully.

questions.text: What is 'Dead Code Elimination' (Tree Shaking) most dependent on in a React setup?
options: The use of TypeScript.
ESM (import/export) static analysis by the bundler.
The number of comments in the code.
Using only functional components.
questions.explanation: Static analysis allows tools like Webpack or Vite to determine which functions are never called and remove them from the production bundle.

questions.text: How should a Lead approach 'Large State Objects' in the Context API?
options: Put everything in one large Context for simplicity.
Split into multiple providers or use a library with 'selectors' to prevent unnecessary re-renders.
Use local storage instead.
Convert the whole app to class components.
questions.explanation: Context re-renders all consumers on any change; splitting providers is a standard lead-level architectural recommendation.

questions.text: What does the 'Interaction to Next Paint' (INP) metric primarily measure in a React application?
options: How fast the first image appears.
The responsiveness of the page to all user interactions over its lifetime.
The time it takes to download the JS bundle.
The size of the HTML file.
questions.explanation: INP replaced FID as a Core Web Vital, focusing on the latency of interactions—highly affected by React's rendering efficiency.
